<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The five wounds are the two in Christ's feet, the two in his hands or wrists, and the one in his side from which flow water and blood at the piercing by the centurion during Jesus' crucifixion.<|18.48|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As it is with St. Thomas from last Sunday, so it is with the apostles this week.<|7.06|>
<|8.18|> The wounds Jesus gains in his crucifixion lend credibility to his post-resurrection body.<|18.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.12|> It's me. It's really me, Jesus says.<|3.80|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Bye.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>

<|16.88|> The well of mercy, the well of grace,<|21.88|>
<|23.30|> the well of comfort, the well of pity, and the well of life.<|27.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.00|> For just a moment or two, we will reflect on these five wounds as we celebrate this third Sunday of Easter.<|9.78|>
<|11.62|> Y'all can tell that I'm very enthusiastic about medieval Christianity.<|16.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> First, the well of mercy.<|2.76|>

<|12.76|> but in some way full and overflowing.<|17.80|>

<|9.28|> but to call sinners.<|10.60|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> which segues quite neatly into the second wound, the well of grace.<|8.00|>

<|17.00|> It is gift. It is free. It is whole.<|23.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this appears to be God's posture towards us and the whole creation, one of gracious goodness.<|10.28|>
<|11.86|> It is by this grace, this gift, that God chooses small, unknown Jacob, wrestles with him by night,<|21.34|>
<|21.34|> changes his name to Israel, and makes his descendants bearers of divine promise.<|28.06|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is by this grace, this gift, that God chooses Mary Magdalene to be the first bearer of resurrection good news, a woman who is not believed when she says, I have seen the Lord.<|16.00|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is by grace that we sit here and pray and celebrate the faithfulness of God towards us<|7.76|>
<|7.76|> and somehow mysteriously this grace is glimpsed in the wounds of Jesus' crucifixion and resurrection.<|16.88|>
<|18.82|> Third, the well of comfort.<|22.34|>

<|14.10|> an end to violence, an end to displacement,<|19.50|>

<|23.80|> the healing of systemic historical wounds in the present.<|29.60|>

<|15.14|> Fourth, the well of pity.<|19.08|>
<|19.08|> The well of pity.<|19.80|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Pity is not really a word we use very often. In one of the Gospels, after teaching all day, Jesus looks out at the crowd and has pity or compassion on them.<|16.30|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This compassion, this pity leads Jesus to Jerusalem and into the stickiness of your life and mine.<|13.18|>
<|15.00|> God's pity leads God into flesh, into creatureliness, into smallness, into us.<|25.86|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|>.<|2.94|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Fifth and finally, the well of life.<|6.42|>
<|6.42|> The fifth wound is the well of life.<|10.06|>
<|10.22|> The apostles, joyful, disbelieving, full of wonder, are given a vision of what life is like in God.<|20.06|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.24|><|endoftext|>

<|20.12|> Notice that after Jesus shows the apostles<|23.64|>
<|23.64|> his wounded hands and feet,<|25.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He asks for broiled fish.<|4.22|>
<|5.92|> And they give it to him and he eats it.<|9.78|>
<|11.14|> Because resurrection offers life not only after death, but it also offers life after life after death.<|20.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or as John Donne, the great 17th century poet says,<|5.42|>
